chunk_summary: Guidance on culturally sensitive complementary feeding, focusing on texture progression, troubleshooting common barriers, and correcting high-risk myths for infants aged 6–23 months.
learning_objectives: Understand the recommended texture progression for complementary feeding from 6–23 months.
Identify strategies to address common feeding challenges, such as food refusal and caregiver fears of choking.
Learn to correct high-risk feeding myths and promote safe, age-appropriate practices.
Incorporate culturally sensitive approaches to meal planning and counselling.
key_facts: From 6–8 months: 2–3 feeds/day of thick, mashed foods with protein (e.g., dal, egg, curd) and added oil/ghee if growth faltering is present.
From 9–11 months: 3–4 feeds/day plus 1–2 snacks, introducing soft lumps and finger foods to develop chewing skills.
From 12–23 months: 3 family meals plus 2 planned snacks, with modified family foods.
Avoid choking hazards like nuts, grapes, hard raw vegetables, and round candies; differentiate gagging from choking.
Do not delay solids beyond 6 months; avoid honey under 12 months and juice/tea as meal substitutes.
reference_quote: Breastmilk (or appropriate formula) continues, but solids become progressively thicker, more frequent, and more textured from 6 months onward. (WHO guideline on complementary feeding of infants and young children 6–23 months of age)
